exemplar.title:
How does the internal pressure (110, 120, 130, 140, 150, 160, 170, 
180, 190, 200, 210, 220) in kPa of a basketball affect its initial rebound 
height when measured with digital recording, and thus its elasticity of 
bounce upon calculation?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How does the internal pressure (110, 120, 130, 140, 150, 160, 170, 
180, 190, 200, 210, 220) in kPa of a basketball affect its initial rebound 
height when measured with digital recording, and thus its elasticity of 
bounce upon calculation?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Uncertainties for s_f, s_i and e are calculated correctly using propagation rules and shown in tables and graphs.
Data processing (unit conversions, ledgeâgap corrections, averaging) is accurate and error bars are applied consistently.
Graphical modelling is competent: fits are justified and RÂ² values reported with high precision.
weaknesses: Justification for using halfârange for s_f and doubling ruler uncertainty is not fully defended.
Horizontal error bars are deemed negligible without quantitative justification.
No discussion of alternative model comparison metrics (AIC, SSE) beyond RÂ².
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands of communication and uncertainty consideration are at band 5 (minor omissions in precision and justification), and processing quality meets band 6. Overall performance aligns with a band 5 holistic score for Data Analysis.

strengths: Research question precisely stated within a specific and appropriate basketball physics context.
Introduction links experiment to Newtonâs law of restitution and published work, demonstrating relevance and specificity.
Apparatus table, annotated diagram and step-by-step method provide sufficient detail for replication.
weaknesses: Pascalâs law is misquoted (P=F/4 instead of P=F/A), indicating a minor conceptual error.
Camera focal distance and ambient temperature control are not specified, limiting full reproducibility.
Inconsistent unit notation (kPA vs kPa) and ambiguous dependentâvariable domain statement need clarification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission meets the topâband descriptor for research question context (band 6) but exhibits minor omissions in methodological detail (camera settings, temperature control) and a small conceptual mistake, placing two methodological strands at band 5. Holistically, this supports awarding 5â/â6 for Research Design.

strengths: Conclusion correctly states that elasticity increases with pressure and plateaus, consistent with the data and model.
Findings are compared to Osman & Kim (2009), linking results to idealâgas behaviour and material properties.
weaknesses: Phrasing around the âprincipal hypothesis still apparentâ is vague and needs clearer linking to data.
Comparison to external study lacks critical evaluation of its statistical reliability (RÂ², uncertainties).
Model selection rationale beyond visual fit is not formally justified.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clearly supported by the analysis (band 5) and the comparison to scientific context is relevant but not deeply justified (band 5). Slight ambiguities prevent band 6, so a holistic score of 5â/â6 is appropriate.

strengths: Evaluation identifies specific methodological weaknesses (alignment, heat buildup, release angle) and links them to data scatter.
Proposed improvements (mechanical drop release, temperature equilibration, multiple cameras) are realistic and relevant.
weaknesses: Lacks quantitative estimates of how much each weakness impacts uncertainty.
Does not include a costâbenefit or resource analysis for implementing the suggested improvements.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both evaluation strands exhibit clear, specific critique and relevant improvements (band 5), but the absence of quantitative impact analysis and detailed feasibility limits this to a band 5 holistic score.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated and situated within the very specific context of basketball design, internal air pressure and the coefficient of restitution.  The introduction links the question to Newtonâs law of restitution, kinetic-energy considerations and to previous published work, demonstrating both relevance and specificity.  No ambiguity exists about what is being investigated or why it matters, satisfying the descriptors for the top band.

Absolute and fractional uncertainties are calculated for sf, si and e; propagation rules for division and powers are applied correctly, and error bars appear on the graphs.  The student justifies doubling ruler uncertainty and acknowledges negligible horizontal error bars.  One assumption (using half-range for sf) is not fully defended, leaving small omissions that cap the strand at 5.

The student explains why each control is needed (material, surface, parallax, etc.) and justifies the pressure range with reference to atmospheric pressure and expected non-bouncing below 110 kPa.  The choice of 12 pressure points and three trials per point yields ample data.  Explanations of parallax practice runs and uncertainty allowances show awareness of sufficiency, though discussion of why three repeats were judged adequate is brief, keeping the performance at 5 rather than 6.

Three specific weaknesses are identified and their impacts on mid-range pressure discrepancies, alignment consistency and recording angles are explained with clear linkage to data scatter or uncertainty.  The reasoning of how residual heat led to systematic error is explicit.  Depth of quantitative impact analysis (e.g. error magnitude estimation) is limited, so a 5 is most fitting.

The step-by-step method, apparatus table and annotated diagram give enough detail for a competent reader to replicate the investigation, including camera frame-rate, exact pressure values, ruler alignment corrections and trial numbers.  Minor gaps remain (e.g. camera focal distance, exact definition of when âmaximum rebound heightâ is picked on the video), so the write-up is marginally short of the faultless clarity implied by 6 marks.

Data processing is accurate: conversion from cm to m, correction for ledge gaps, averaging of trials, derivation of e from SUVAT, and propagation of uncertainties are all carried out without detectable numerical errors.  Graphical modelling (cubic vs logarithmic) is handled competently, demonstrating fully appropriate and accurate processing.

Each weakness is paired with a realistic, relevant improvement (room-temperature pre-softened tube, mechanical drop release, multiple cameras, alternative ball choice).  Explanations clarify how each suggestion would enhance reliability or validity.  While feasible, resource implications and expected quantitative benefit are not fully elaborated, aligning the work with band 5 rather than 6.

Raw and processed data tables carry headings, units and uncertainties; graphs include error bars, fitted equations and RÂ² values.  A few typographical issues (e.g. inconsistent capitalisation of units, some table mis-alignment) detract slightly from precision but clarity is maintained throughout, meriting a solid 5.

The student compares findings to Osman & Kim (2009) and interprets similarities/differences via ideal-gas behaviour and wall thickness.  The discussion is relevant and reasoned but does not deeply critique the external studyâs methodology or statistical quality, hence falls slightly short of the comprehensive justification characteristic of full-mark work.

The conclusion correctly states that elasticity rises with pressure and that the relationship plateaus, matching the processed data and fitted model.  It references the asymptote below e = 1 and explicitly links back to the hypothesis.  Minor wording ambiguity about âprincipal hypothesis is still apparentâ prevents the absolute consistency expected for 6 marks.

annotations.comment: The research question is precisely stated and situated within basketball physics, linking pressure, rebound height and elasticity clearly.
Title conveys a focused, specific context by mentioning internal pressure and elasticity in basketballs, setting a clear investigation scope.
The definition of coefficient of restitution is clear, with correct notation $$e=\frac{v_f}{v_i}$$ and clear bounds, supporting readersâ understanding.
Introduction situates the experiment in published work and physical theory, framing the research question within a detailed scientific context.
Pascalâs law is misquoted as $$P=F/4$$; it should read $$P=F/A$$. Correct this to avoid conceptual errors in the hypothesis.
Figure 1 effectively visualizes the expected trend, reinforcing the hypothesis with a clear sketch of an asymptote.
The dependent-variable domain statement 0<y<2.3535 is mathematically correct but needs clarification of the physical assumptions behind the upper bound.
Inconsistent unit notation appears (kPA vs kPa). Ensure SI units are used consistently throughout.
The drop-height rowâs error classification âprevented theoretical systematicâ is vague; specify whether itâs a systematic or random uncertainty.
The apparatus list is comprehensive, detailing instruments and quantities clearly for reproducibility.
The method description omits the cameraâs focal distance and lighting conditions; include these to improve reproducibility.
Safety considerations are well covered, demonstrating awareness of practical hazards and suggesting control measures.
Preliminary testing paragraph clearly justifies practice drops to minimize parallax error.
Uncertainty table applies half-range for $$s_f$$ without justification; explain why maxâmin/2 is appropriate for this setup.
Raw data are well tabulated, but the bolded 130 kPa entry lacks explanation; clarify the significance of formatting.
The explanation of the ledge offset and ruler gap is precise, showing awareness of systematic corrections.
Derivation of $$e=\sqrt{\frac{s_f}{s_i}}$$ via SUVAT is correctly presented and easy to follow.
Uncertainty formula $$\Delta s_f=\frac{\max-min}{2}$$ is applied correctly, demonstrating solid uncertainty analysis.
Doubling ruler uncertainty lacks clear rationale; quantify alignment and reading errors separately rather than blanket doubling.
The application of the uncertainty rule for powers is correct: $$\frac{\Delta y}{y}=|n\frac{\Delta a}{a}|$$ for $$a^n$$.
Processed data table shows accurate averages, propagated uncertainties and resulting $$e$$ values, enabling transparent data analysis.
Cubic regression with $$R^2=0.9986$$ is well justified, indicating almost perfect fit between pressure and elasticity.
Consider quantitatively fitting an exponential or logarithmic model alongside the cubic to compare goodness-of-fit metrics (e.g., AIC, SSE).
Conclusion correctly notes the plateau but phrasing âprincipal hypothesis still apparentâ is vague; specify how results confirm or modify the hypothesis.
Comparison to Osman & Kim enhances context but lacks critique of their statistical reliability; consider discussing their $$R^2$$ and error bars.
The alternative logarithmic model is well presented but formal criteria for model selection (beyond visual fit) should be used.
Evaluation table identifies specific weaknesses linking method steps to data scatter, demonstrating sound critical reflection.
Suggested mechanical drop release is feasible but would benefit from a costâbenefit discussion regarding implementation.
Recommendation to use a different ball material is relevant but should include a discussion of expected quantitative impact on the trend.
Ambient temperature was not monitored in the setup; recording room temperature would control for gasâpressure variations.
Horizontal error bars are deemed negligible but their magnitude should still be reported to justify omission.
Model section lacks discussion of alternative fit metrics such as AIC or SEE; include these for robust model evaluation.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
